food_subgroup: One-pot soft rice and moong dal porridge

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Khichdi. Nearest parent is a cooked rice-lentil porridge. A new concept for Indian one-pot rice-legume porridges is recommended.
ontology.foodex2_description: Soft cooked rice and moong dal porridge, Indian style (closest parent)

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of khichdi is 1 bowl (approximately 300g) — a smooth, slightly thick, pourable porridge of completely soft white rice and moong dal, delicately flavoured with turmeric and cumin ghee tempering.

nutrient_source.title: Khichdi — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, derived from white rice and moong dal composition data
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN does not have a standalone plain khichdi entry. Values derived from ICMR-NIN cooked white rice and split moong dal data per standard khichdi recipe proportions (rice 40%, moong dal 20%, water ~38%, ghee/turmeric ~2%). Cross-referenced with pongal data as a comparable preparation.

gi_source: University of Sydney GI Database — estimated from soft-cooked rice and moong dal mixture; the moong dal lowers GI vs plain cooked rice; the very soft porridge consistency raises GI slightly vs firm-cooked rice.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium approximately 426mg per bowl (300g) — moderate; khichdi is naturally low in sodium and can be prepared with minimal salt.
hypertension.guidance: Excellent choice for hypertensive patients — one of the lowest-sodium complete meals in Indian cuisine. The high water content and mild preparation make it ideal for patients requiring bland, easy-to-digest food.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate GI (~65); glycaemic load per bowl (300g) is approximately 34. The moong dal provides protein and fibre that meaningfully lowers the GI compared to plain rice porridge.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Khichdi is suitable for diabetic patients as a comfort/convalescent meal — limit to 200g portions. The moong dal content provides protein and fibre that moderate the glycaemic response.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Moong dal is the easiest legume to digest and has very low purine content; turmeric provides curcumin; ghee provides butyrate. Traditionally considered the most anti-inflammatory and gut-healing Indian food.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Khichdi is excellent for patients with rheumatoid arthritis, particularly during flares — it is extremely easy to digest, anti-inflammatory (turmeric, ghee), and nutritionally complete as a light meal.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Khichdi is India's original comfort food — a simple, soothing one-pot porridge of white rice and split yellow moong dal cooked together with water, turmeric, cumin seeds, and a small amount of ghee until the rice and dal lose their individual structure and merge into a smooth, slightly pourable, pale-yellow porridge. It is the most widely prescribed therapeutic food in Indian dietary practice — recommended for convalescence, upset stomachs, fevers, and recovery. Nutritionally, it provides a complete amino acid profile from the rice-dal combination, with very low sodium and easy digestibility. Plain khichdi is deeply humble in flavour and deeply healing in effect.

confidence_reason: No standalone plain khichdi entry in ICMR-NIN or HPB Foodo. Values derived from ICMR-NIN white rice and moong dal component data per standard recipe proportions. The high water content (~75%) makes caloric estimates reliable.
notes: Contains dairy (ghee) — not vegan or dairy-free when made with ghee. Vegan when made with oil. Cholesterol approximately 12mg per bowl (300g) when made with ghee. Khichdi prepared with more dal has a lower GI and higher protein content. Vegetable additions (spinach, peas, ginger) significantly improve the micronutrient profile.
